title: Deep litter method for coop management
subtitle: Building a self-composting floor in a chicken coop to cut mucking-out frequency through the winter months.
excerpt: A deep-litter floor, managed correctly, can go three to four months between full changes, generating its own heat through microbial activity and providing a dust-bathing medium. It requires a minimum coop area and a specific regime of aeration and top-dressing. Get the balance wrong and it compacts into an anaerobic mat that generates ammonia and disease.
sourceNotes: DEFRA, Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Laying Hens (2002), litter management sections. https://www.gov.uk

content.text: method is best suited to coops with a minimum floor area of around 1.5 m², solid walls, and good roof ventilation. It works poorly in small, low, damp hutches where moisture cannot escape. The method suits a static coop used by a stable flock through winter; a mobile ark cleaned out weekly is simpler management.

Start with a clean, dry floor. Lay down 10 to 15 cm of dry material: wood shavings, straw, or a mix. Hemp bedding holds moisture well and composts cleanly but costs more than shavings. Do not use sawdust, which compacts into an anaerobic pan under chicken traffic. The first layer is the composting base: it should feel springy underfoot, not hard.

is the primary management tool. Do it every two weeks: crouch at hen height in the closed coop first thing in the morning. No smell is good. A faint background smell means the litter needs aerating with a fork and a top-dress of fresh dry material. A sharp eye-stinging smell means the bed is failing and needs partial replacement.

In good conditions, add 5 cm of fresh material every three to four weeks and turn the top layer with a fork to aerate it. The chickens themselves do much of the turning: scatter scratch grain in the litter daily and they will work it over. The bed should feel warm a few centimetres below the surface, a sign of healthy microbial activity.

A well-managed deep-litter bed in a good-sized coop can run from October to February or March. Remove the whole bed in spring and compost it for six months before using it on the garden: fresh chicken manure is too nitrogen-rich and contains pathogens that hot composting eliminates. Wash and dry the coop floor before starting a new bed.
